name: Alban Berg
abstract: Alban Maria Johannes Berg ( BAIRG; Austrian German: [ˈalbaːn ˈbɛrg]; 9 February 1885 – 24 December 1935) was an Austrian composer of the Second Viennese School. His compositional style combined Romantic lyricism with the twelve-tone technique.

about_quotes.speaker: Branford Marsalis
about_quotes.evidence: I know Lulu... That’s not Berg, by the way. He’s classically trained. And he wrote in very traditional styles... But, when you listen to Berg... There’s a melody line throughout all of it that can be followed
about_quotes.doc_title: Branford Marsalis — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Roger Kellaway
about_quotes.evidence: I’ve listened to much too much twentieth century classical music... because there’s too much Webern and Alban Berg and Prokofiev and Stravinsky... that have shaped my thinking about what sound is about.
about_quotes.doc_title: Roger Kellaway interviewed by Monk Rowe, Los Angeles, California, February 14, 1999

about_quotes.speaker: Dan Morgenstern
about_quotes.evidence: But he became a close friend of Alban Berg so I remember Berg, who died when I was just six years old. But I remember him well because he was a close friend, and sometimes my father and he would take me to — well they were soccer fans and would go to soccer games.
about_quotes.doc_title: Dan Morgenstern interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, April 9, 2013

about_quotes.speaker: Noah Evans
about_quotes.evidence: I haven't analysed the parts of the album very good that I like very much so, the parts of Webern that I like very much. Some of them may be done by that strictly, but they were both students of Schoenberg.

about_quotes.speaker: Dan Morgenstern
about_quotes.evidence: Alban Berg – who was a very close friend of my father’s and whom I saw a lot also – was one of the few people in Vienna among my father’s circle who had a car.
about_quotes.doc_title: Dan Morgenstern — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Mandel Johnny
about_quotes.evidence: it was sort of an Alban Berg type of approach to atonality, using rows, but it was very sensual music like Berg’s was, or is.
about_quotes.doc_title: Mandel Johnny — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
